chapters.title: The hidden history of garments
chapters.text:
Have you ever wondered what happened to your jeans before you bought them? What cotton was used? Where was it spun, turned into fabric, and dyed? Who worked on it, and under what conditions? Labels won't provide us with answers. Why? Many companies are only merchant brands that design, advertise, and sell. They don't know about the other phases of jeans production or don't want to.

How is it possible? Once manufacturing moved to Asia in the 1990s, responsibility for production shifted to a chain of intermediaries. As a result, consumers can't be sure that their garments aren't made at factories that violate human rights or use toxic ingredients. Even famous companies have been accused of exploiting overseas workers.

Transparency about all stages of garment production is the basis of ethical fashion.

To change the current state of affairs in the textile industry and the economic system that enables it, we should first discover the full story behind our clothes. Are you ready? Join this eye-opening investigation and learn practical solutions for responsible production and consumption.

chapters.title: Fruits you wear daily
chapters.text:
To make our journey into the fashion industry more specific, we will focus on jeans: customers buy around 1.25 billion annually. This garment's path starts with cotton shrubs, which are cultivated in two ways:

• Conventional: Farmers use fertilizers, insecticides, and herbicides to attain the maximum harvest at minimum cost. As a result, a pair of jeans made from conventional cotton contains around 340 grams of chemicals, which can negatively affect our health.
• Organic: In this case, farmers apply classic methods, like manually picking weeds and crop rotation. It's not a lucrative business, as organic cotton costs almost the same as conventional, but risks and expenses are higher. Yet, it helps restore the soil and provides chemical-free cotton (0.7% of all produced) and non-genetically modified cotton seeds.

Support those who prioritize safety and ecology over profitability.

After fruits (cotton bolls) are ready for harvesting, special vehicles collect and compress them into huge loaves that weigh over 11,000 kilograms. Then, farmers transport these loaves to cotton gins to separate fibers from seeds and later to exchanges, where intermediaries organize the rest. Farmers rarely know where their cotton will travel afterward and who will buy it, reducing their profits and blurring responsibility for final results.

Pesticides aren't the only chemicals our jeans contain. The next production stage adds new ones. After cotton arrives in factories, usually in Asian countries with low labor costs, fibers are cleaned, spun into yarn, and woven into a textile. Afterward, fabrics are immersed in chemicals that improve their quality, make them wrinkle-free, and dye them specific colors. Indeed, textiles are washed afterward, but it doesn't remove all the chemicals; generally, they comprise 28% of natural fabrics. Synthetic ones, made from oil, contain even more. What are their effects?

• Our skin soaks these chemicals.
• Washed-out waste from textile factories appears in rivers and oceans, contaminating our food and water.
• When we wash synthetic fabrics at home, microplastic sheds, increasing the amount of plastic in the environment.

Moreover, turning fibers into textiles requires a lot of energy. It usually comes from fossil fuels, resulting in contaminated air.

Did you know? 94% of plastic in oceans is undetectable by the human eye; it's microplastic.

chapters.title: Brands as new governments
chapters.text:
While fabrics are often created in China, the cut-and-sew stage mostly unfolds in other countries. Why? The strong Chinese economy allows citizens to choose workplaces, and arduous clothing production isn't among their preferences. Many other countries are less lucky. For instance, Bangladesh depends totally on the textile industry. Indeed, it helped the country reduce poverty and increase the average lifespan, but the cost is high.

A typical Bangladesh factory has three flows — cutting, sewing, and packaging. The work speed is unbelievable: a row of 66 people sew 161 pants per hour. Each detail, such as a belt loop or button, is attached by hand. If anyone drops pace, for instance, by sneezing or scratching a nose, managers threaten or physically assault them. Also, workers can't visit restrooms without permission, and the doors to the factory are closed, which is illegal. It's no surprise that people feel locked in a cage.

Do workers earn at least a minimum wage? No. Why? That's where the dark side of dependence on brands comes in. To keep companies in Bangladesh, the government sets minimum wages lower than they should be. Scientists call this process hyperglobalization — when brands become more influential than authorities. With many countries to choose from, companies can lower prices or change design at the last minute, and no one will protest.

Diversifying your income is the path to financial independence.

What can we do to promote fairness in the fashion industry?

• Force the companies to change: Brands already feel our pressure, hiring monitoring companies to check whether chosen factories have decent work conditions. Still, this measure is ineffective: no matter the result, factories have no sanctions or benefits. That's why the government should ensure real audits and prohibit importing goods that violate human rights. If brands mistreat factories or work with ones that exploit laborers, fines should be extremely high. Yet, to encourage authorities to promote fairness in the fashion industry, we must first limit business lobbying, as some companies spend up to $16 million on it.
• Adjust prices: Research proves that if a brand raises the price of T-shirts by 12-25 cents and gives it to factory workers, their lives will improve tremendously.

chapters.title: Needs vs. desires culture
chapters.text:
Have you ever wondered whether our wishes to buy something are truly ours? In fact, different factors, like French King Louis XIV's reforms, may cause them.

When he came to power in the 17th century, European politics revolved around Spain. Louis XIV aimed to make France a superpower instead and chose fashion as his weapon. He immensely invested in the textile industry and started the tradition of a fashion season. It was a political trick. Stating that some clothes were acceptable only for a limited period, Louis XIV prompted other European countries to look to a new fashion capital — Paris.

Aim to develop your style; don’t just keep up with trends.

Another factor that influences our buying habits takes root in the post-WWII period. Numerous industries had to adjust to peacetime. While bomb factories shifted to producing fertilizers, as nitrogen was used in both cases, others switched to making clothes or fridges, and their biggest fear was overproduction.

To prevent it, some executives offered to turn the US into a desires culture, promoting consumption. In a way, brands managed to do it. Their marketing has equaled buying things with feeling better and encourages people to try something new through influencers. So, next time, before clicking "buy" on Amazon, the US's largest retailer of clothes, let's ask: "Do I really need this garment?"

If the answer is yes, and the order is placed, clothes move to another stage — delivering. The size of the Amazon fulfillment center can reach 16 football fields. So, after workers leave all their belongings in lockers, making them unavailable even for urgent calls, they walk up to 15 miles daily to fill orders. It's an arduous job: only workers in their 20s and 30s can keep up with the rhythm. Yet, they earn around $28,000 annually, which forces them to file for food stamps. Unions can improve the situation, but many laborers don't consider joining them, fearing dismissal.

How did business get to this point? Initially, corporations adhered to a stakeholder model that included workers and the public's interests. However, in the 1970s, economist Milton Friedman offered a shareholder primacy model central to neoliberalism. It replaced business' social responsibilities with making profits.

If we want to preserve the benefits of capitalism, we must purge it of neoliberal extremism. ~ Maxine Bédat

chapters.title: The flip side of donations
chapters.text:
In 2017, 12.8 million tons of clothes and shoes in the US ended up in landfills. Natural fibers break down, releasing methane gas, greatly contributing to global warming, while synthetics will stay in the soil for thousands of years. To prevent garment wastage, many US authorities encourage donations. Although it seems like a better way, it also has pitfalls.

Donation stores don’t usually distribute garments to those with low incomes or homeless people. They select market-worthy pieces, sell them as secondhand (20%), and spend collected money on charitable purposes. The rest, 80% of the clothes, move to the next stage — grading, which usually happens overseas, where workers classify unsold donations depending on their future purposes:

• Secondhand (45%)
• Commercial or industrial rags (30%)
• Reprocessed fabrics, such as furniture stuffing (20%)
• Trash (5%)

Packed in huge bales, secondhand clothes are shipped to markets like Kantamanto in Ghana. These markets are also destinations for other types of textiles, such as unsold apparel from brands and garments from store give-back programs. Unfortunately, 50-60% of the clothes we bring to shops are exported overseas.

Learn to distinguish eco-friendly initiatives from merely marketing ones.

At Kantamanto, sellers buy bales without knowing what's inside, which is a grave risk. If the bale lacks new garments or ones in a decent state, the sellers may go broke as customers won't buy their items. On average, for every two bought apparel, three end up in landfills, contaminating local soil, water, and air (due to fires).

Some argue that selling secondhand in low-income countries creates many jobs, while others believe it resembles dependence rather than fair trade. Only importers who ship these clothes to Africa can earn up to $100,000 weekly. Sellers get much less: a good bale can bring around $66 of gross profit, while a bale with clothes that nobody will buy results in a $165 loss. The kayayei, women who earn their living by transporting 55-70 kg bales on their heads to retailers' stalls, are in the least favorable position. Despite their arduous work, they receive less than $9 per week.

This dependence decreases now: COVID-19 tipped the scales to Ghanaian designers and producers. Yet, a long-term solution requires profound reforms in the fashion industry and buying habits of the West because 65% of secondhand comes from Europe and the US.

chapters.title: Your choices make a difference
chapters.text:
The fashion industry must change, and we can encourage it to do so using two superpowers. The first one, the power of the purse, comprises simple steps that make a big change, such as:

• Analyze your closet: Once you determine what you wear most often, you know what to buy and eliminate from your wardrobe. Yet, instead of discarding or donating unnecessary clothes, remind yourself of the efforts involved in these garments and devise ways to reuse them. For instance, you can lend or remake these items with a tailor's help.
• Limit returning clothes: Although buying a few sizes of garments and returning ones that don't fit seems practical, it increases the number of clothes that end up in landfills. Brands rarely create departments responsible for ironing and repackaging returned garments. More often, they discard these clothes.
• Avoid falling into slogans: Sustainable fashion is essential, but we need more research data about different stages of production to call some brands truly sustainable.
• Resist social media trends: Limiting time online is an effective way to avoid triggers that lure you into accumulating more. Remember that influencers earn money on your purchases. Each time you use their promo code, they receive a commission.
• Buy intentionally: If a purchase is unavoidable, ensure you've chosen the right piece. For instance, ask yourself where you will wear it and whether you can combine it with other garments from your closet. Also, learn more about your chosen brands, including who they work with and their values. Indeed, discovering this information can be challenging, but small brands are more transparent.

Citizen engagement is the foundation for social reform.

Using the power of our voices, we can make eco-friendly fashion a reality. Fighting the exploitation of people and resources may seem daunting, but it's possible. The top 20 brands receive over 95% of fashion profits, so if politicians prompt them to change, the effects will be profound. What reforms are needed?

• Transparency of the production process
• Decrease in carbon emissions
• Control of used chemicals
• Decent wages and conditions for all workers, including those overseas

Signing petitions, joining demonstrations, and voting for politicians immune to business lobbying can bring noticeable results.

The United States is the largest market, and because of this we have a lot of leverage. ~ Maxine Bédat

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Reports show that the fashion industry produces 100-150 billion new garments annually. With this scope, it has become a potent force that shapes our environment, economics, and the lives of millions of people. So, its greater transparency is vital for the health of our planet and ourselves. What should we aim for?

• Regenerative practices of agriculture during fiber creation
• Safe chemicals at the textile production level
• Decent working conditions at sewing factories and warehouses
• Recycling of textiles we no longer need

Implementing these changes will benefit the fabric of our society.

Try this

• Reflect on your style and use these observations as filters for future shopping.
• Search for local brands that design and manufacture clothes in your region or country. Try to support those who care about the environment and work conditions.
• Wear clothes from natural fabrics more often.

Read it till the end?
